text: The surgical setup includes four trocars placed approximately fifteen centimeters below the costal margin: one 12mm port, three 8mm ports, and a 5mm port for the Nathanson retractor.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to achieve hemostasis along the greater curvature of the stomach, minimizing thermal spread while isolating the stomach in preparation for sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon examines the angle of His, a critical area in sleeve gastrectomies where leaks commonly occur. An incidental hiatal hernia is visible, which was not detected during preoperative endoscopy. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of identifying and repairing hiatal hernias to prevent reflux and isolate potential sleeve leaks within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon delicately identifies the V-shaped cruz of the diaphragm, distinguishing the left and right diaphragm using blunt dissection. The robotic vessel sealer is used sparingly to preserve anatomical structures, particularly avoiding injury to the vagal nerves. A 36 French suction bougie is placed to aid in identifying the stomach anatomy and positioning for future stapling.

The bougie with suction capability is placed, aiding in the visualization of the esophagus and the identification of the left and right curl fibers. The surgeon uses Ethavon suture to re-approximate the curl fibers, repairing the hiatal hernia.

The surgeon uses Ethabon suture to re-approximate the curl fibers, carefully forming slip knots with a large needle driver to avoid excessive tension that could damage the fibers or cause ischemia. Vessel sealer scissors are used to cut the suture, efficiently bringing the curl fibers together without additional robotic scissors.

The surgeon checks and repositions the esophagus, stomach, and bougie to ensure the repair is not overly tight, avoiding dysphagia. The focus is on re-approximating the tissue with controlled suturing while minimizing unnecessary steps.

The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to cut the suture and manipulate the tissue. After placing the last suture, the surgeon grabs the suture with the vessel sealer and removes it, ensuring the instrument tips remain closed during exchange.

The surgeon identifies the posterior side of the stomach and frees the area to ensure a precise staple line. The bougie is positioned towards the lesser curvature of the stomach, aiding in the manipulation and traction necessary for effective stapling. Excess fat or tissue in the area is identified for removal.

The surgeon begins stapling approximately four centimeters from the pylorus using a robotic stapler. The SureForm stapler is employed, which senses tissue thickness to ensure the appropriate load is selected. The procedure starts with a green load and progresses to a blue load for stapling the stomach tissue.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to ensure the staple lines are positioned next to the bougie without causing constriction, aiming for a smooth anatomical contour of the sleeve to prevent dysphagia.

The surgeon checks the blood supply of the stomach, ensuring it is healthy, viable, and pink, while separating the stomach and pushing it to the side. The final staples are prepared for removal, with constant monitoring above and below to avoid excessive tightness on the stomach.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative gastroscopy to visually inspect the interior of the stomach, checking for bleeding and ensuring the stomach's contour is smooth without abnormalities or strictures. After the inspection, the surgeon suctions any residual blood using a modified NG tube and applies a fibrin glue sealant.

The surgeon cuts the stomach at the ten centimeter mark using robotic arms for precision control. A fibrin sealant is applied for additional hemostasis and to maintain the stomach's position, preventing torsion. The stomach is removed through a twelve millimeter incision, which is closed using a zero-vicryl suture and a Carter-Thompson device to re-approximate the peritoneum and fascia. The Nathanson retractor is removed.
